dialogue_exemplars: All right, Miles. Let me tell you everything you THINK you know. I was married to Rex for a long time. I was an excellent wife, a partner, a lover, a hostess and a friend. There was only one thing I did wrong during the five years we were together. I got five years older. Think he should be able to ditch me for that?

I don't have to. That's what the IRS guys do. And they do it with great zeal. See, they work at these tortuous civil service jobs, and when five hundred dollar an hour boys like you take their trade out in luxury goodies, these saps feel.. well, they feel like saps. And they feel bitter and they feel vengeful and they feel WRATH.
Dismiss your vows, your feigned tears, your flattery, for where a heart is hard, they make no battery.

No. You listen to me. (Very quiet and deliberate) You busted me, Miles. You left me with nothing! What did you expect me to do? Get a degree in counseling? Write a book about table linen? Because that's what wives do when they get dumped, and frankly, I'm not quite ready for that.
Who ever lov'd that lov'd not at first sight?
I think it's only fair to warn you: I'm going to file an action, Miles. And after a decent interval I plan to have Ruth seek an injunction that will forbid your approach within feet of my house.
Therefore in an effort to remove any trace of suspicion from my sweet Howard -- I wish to execute a pre-nuptial agreement.

I don't blame them for betraying me. I don't blame Rex, or Howard or my father. You see, Miles, I'm going to tell you something about me. Something
you may or may not know. I suck!

You really think I engineered the whole thing. You think the marriage and the divorce was part of some scheme. You came here to celebrate because you think I'm without morality or soul. You -- (With difficulty) sound like my mother.
Miles. Miles. Miles. MILES
Anyway, even Rex seemed perplexed by his intransigence. If I didn't know better, I'd swear Massey had some personal investment in my ruination.

It was like that scene in The Godfather. Frankie Pentangeli is called to testify against the Family. And he's in court, and he looks into the spectators gallery, and sees his Brother. They brought the brother from Sicily. And Frankie can't say a word. He can't testify. That's what it was like seeing Pat in there. I couldn't even have Ruth cross examine her.
Miles. Listen to me. You are about to become my husband. I don't want to be protected from you. I want to be protected for you.
Well, I suppose I could "girly" it up for you with a little Fortuny, and some passementerie --

You get out of the car. That's right,
Fuctard. I'm talkin' to you!

relation_context.relation_summary: Miles Massey and Marylin share a complex, evolving relationship defined by intense romantic passion and deep emotional connection, repeatedly interrupted by legal and personal conflict. Their bond begins with mutual professional admiration and immediate romantic attraction, evidenced by exchanged glances, verbal flirtation, and declarations of love at first sight. This evolves into a full romantic and marital relationship: they propose, marry, share intimate domestic routines (breakfast, movie nights, home decoration), and express desires for children and lifelong commitment. Key events such as Miles giving Marylin a credit card to 'Marylinize' their home, their morning kisses, and collaborative escape from danger underscore deep emotional intimacy and partnership. However, their relationship is simultaneously undermined by their roles as legal adversaries—Miles initially represents Rex in Marylin’s divorce, creating a conflict of interest that resurfaces even after their marriage. This duality erupts into open hostility: they accuse each other of hiring goons, engage in courtroom confrontations, exchange derogatory language, and experience bitter emotional breakdowns, including mutual accusations of betrayal and moral corruption. Despite these conflicts, their romantic feelings persist; they repeatedly reconcile, share kisses, and affirm their love, culminating in a final recognition that they have 'always been in love.' The relationship is thus cyclical—oscillating between passionate union and destructive confrontation—shaped by both genuine affection and professional entanglement. Their dynamic profoundly influences their decisions: Miles abandons legal objectivity for personal desire, while Marylin alternates between seeking protection from and offering loyalty to Miles. The evidence confirms a transformation from adversaries to lovers, then to spouses, and back to conflicted ex-spouses who retain deep emotional ties.
